docs.title: Psynapse
docs.text: A cross-platform node-based UI editor for building AI workflows using Python.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
Psynapse provides a simple and intuitive interface to build and deploy AI workflows using Python using a an intuitive node-based editor. The biggest strength of Psynapse lies in the following 2 aspects:

  1. Its written entirely in Python, making it easy to extend, customize and integrate with other Python libraries.
  2. Developing your own nodepacks for AI libraries in the Python ecosystem is as easy as writing a simple Python function with type hints for parameters and return values.
  3. The decoupled execution runtime allows you to run your workflows on a remote server or GPU clusters, while running the editor on your local machine.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: uv pip install -U git+https://github.com/soumik12345/psynapse.git

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Simply run the command psynapse in the terminal, this would launch the Psynapse application with a locally hosted execution runtime.

docs.title: Run with a remote backend
docs.text:
1. Start the execution runtime locally or in a remote VM using

    psynapse-backend --port 8000

    The backend will be available at http://localhost:8000.

  2. Launch the Psynapse editor using

    psynapse --backend-port 8000

docs.title: Sample Workflows
docs.text:
• OpenAI Workflows
  • LiteLLM Workflows

docs.title: Acknowledgments
docs.text: Psynapse is heavily inspired by Nodezator and ComfyUI.

docs.title: Editor (editor.py)
docs.text:
• Main application window
  • Manages node library and canvas
  • Coordinates execution via Run button
  • Displays status and errors

docs.title: Node View (view.py)
docs.text:
• Interactive canvas for node graph
  • Handles pan, zoom, and connections
  • Drag-and-drop support

docs.title: Serializer (serializer.py)
docs.text:
• Converts UI graph to JSON
  • Maps node classes to backend types
  • Serializes sockets and connections

docs.title: Backend Client (backend_client.py)
docs.text:
• HTTP client for FastAPI backend
  • Async/sync method wrappers
  • Connection health checks

docs.title: Server (server.py)
docs.text:
• REST API endpoints
  • CORS configuration
  • Request validation

docs.title: Node Schemas (node_schemas.py)
docs.text:
• Defines available node types
  • Specifies parameters and returns
  • Type information

docs.title: Executor (executor.py)
docs.text:
• Graph execution engine
  • Dependency resolution
  • Result caching
  • Error handling

docs.title: Graph Serialization
docs.text:
Input (UI Nodes): 

nodes = [AddNode(), ViewNode()]
# AddNode has inputs A=5.0, B=3.0
# ViewNode connected to AddNode output

Output (JSON): 

{
  "nodes": [
    {
      "id": "node_0",
      "type": "add",
      "input_sockets": [
        {"id": "node_0_input_0", "name": "a", "value": 5.0},
        {"id": "node_0_input_1", "name": "b", "value": 3.0}
      ],
      "output_sockets": [
        {"id": "node_0_output_0", "name": "result"}
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "node_1",
      "type": "view",
      "input_sockets": [
        {"id": "node_1_input_0", "name": "value", "value": null}
      ],
      "output_sockets": []
    }
  ],
  "edges": [
    {
      "start_socket": "node_0_output_0",
      "end_socket": "node_1_input_0"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Execution Results
docs.text:
Backend Response: 

{
  "results": {
    "node_1": {
      "value": 8.0,
      "error": null
    }
  }
}

Frontend Processing:

  • Maps node_1 → second ViewNode in graph

  • Updates ViewNode display to show 8.0

  • Clears any error state

docs.title: Psynapse Backend
docs.text: The Psynapse backend is a FastAPI server that handles node graph execution. This decouples the compute from the UI, allowing for more scalable and testable node execution.

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
1. FastAPI Server (psynapse/backend/server.py)
  2. Provides REST API endpoints for node operations

  3. Handles graph execution requests

  4. Node Schemas (psynapse/backend/node_schemas.py)

  5. Defines the schema for available nodes

  6. Specifies parameters and return types for each node

  7. Graph Executor (psynapse/backend/executor.py)

  8. Executes node graphs
  9. Manages node dependencies and caching
  10. Returns results for ViewNodes

docs.title: Frontend Integration
docs.text:
1. Graph Serializer (psynapse/core/serializer.py)
  2. Converts the UI node graph to a JSON format

  3. Serializes nodes and edges for backend consumption

  4. Backend Client (psynapse/editor/backend_client.py)

  5. HTTP client for communicating with the backend
  6. Provides sync wrappers for async operations

docs.title: GET /nodes
docs.text:
Returns the schema of all available nodes.

Response: 

{
  "nodes": [
    {
      "name": "add",
      "params": [
        {"name": "a", "type": "float"},
        {"name": "b", "type": "float"}
      ],
      "returns": [
        {"name": "result", "type": "float"}
      ]
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: POST /execute
docs.text:
Executes a node graph and returns results.

Request: 

{
  "nodes": [
    {
      "id": "node_0",
      "type": "add",
      "input_sockets": [
        {"id": "node_0_input_0", "name": "a", "value": 5.0},
        {"id": "node_0_input_1", "name": "b", "value": 3.0}
      ],
      "output_sockets": [
        {"id": "node_0_output_0", "name": "result"}
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "node_1",
      "type": "view",
      "input_sockets": [
        {"id": "node_1_input_0", "name": "value", "value": null}
      ],
      "output_sockets": []
    }
  ],
  "edges": [
    {
      "start_socket": "node_0_output_0",
      "end_socket": "node_1_input_0"
    }
  ]
}

Response: 

{
  "results": {
    "node_1": {
      "value": 8.0,
      "error": null
    }
  }
}

docs.title: GET /health
docs.text:
Health check endpoint.

Response: 

{
  "status": "ok"
}

docs.title: Method 1: Using the helper script
docs.text: uv run python start_backend.py

docs.title: Method 2: Using uvicorn directly
docs.text: uvicorn psynapse.backend.server:app --reload

docs.title: Method 3: Using uvicorn with custom host/port
docs.text: uvicorn psynapse.backend.server:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

docs.title: Usage Workflow
docs.text:
1. Start the backend server 

    uv run python start_backend.py

  2. Launch the Psynapse editor 

    uv run psynapse

  3. Build your node graph

  4. Drag nodes from the library panel

  5. Connect nodes by dragging from output to input sockets

  6. Execute the graph

  7. Click the "▶ Run Graph" button
  8. The frontend will serialize the graph and send it to the backend
  9. Results will be displayed in ViewNodes

docs.title: Adding New Node Types
docs.text:
To add a new node type:

  1. Add the node schema in psynapse/backend/node_schemas.py: 

    {
    "name": "new_node",
    "params": [
        {"name": "param1", "type": "float"},
    ],
    "returns": [
        {"name": "result", "type": "float"},
    ],
}

  2. Implement the execution logic in psynapse/backend/executor.py: 

    elif node_type == "new_node":
    param1 = float(inputs.get("param1", 0.0))
    return some_computation(param1)

  3. Create the frontend node class in psynapse/nodes/: 

    class NewNode(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(
            title="New Node",
            inputs=[("Param1", SocketDataType.FLOAT)],
            outputs=[("Result", SocketDataType.FLOAT)],
        )

  4. Register the node in the serializer mapping (psynapse/core/serializer.py): 

    NODE_TYPE_MAP = {
    NewNode: "new_node",
    # ... other nodes
}

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Test the backend endpoints using curl or any HTTP client:

# Health check
curl http://localhost:8000/health

# Get node schemas
curl http://localhost:8000/nodes

# Execute a graph
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/execute \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"nodes": [...], "edges": [...]}'

docs.title: LiteLLM LLM Response
docs.text:
This workflow demonstrates how to use the LiteLLM to generate a response to a user's message.

Download Workflow

Note
  • Make sure that you have the respective environment variable set in preferences.
  • Make sure that the output mode in the Text Node is set to LiteLLM Content.
  • The exact index to access the response from a non-reasoning OpenAI response is ['choices'][0]['message']['content']. This is the value that you need to pass to the query with index node.

docs.title: LiteLLM Vision Response
docs.text:
This workflow demonstrates how to use the LiteLLM to generate a response to a user's message to use the vision capabilities of an LLM.

Download Workflow

Note
  • Make sure that you have the respective environment variable set in preferences.
  • Make sure that the output mode in the Text and Image Nodes is set to LiteLLM Content.
  • The exact index to access the response from a non-reasoning OpenAI response is ['choices'][0]['message']['content']. This is the value that you need to pass to the query with index node.

docs.title: OpenAI LLM Response
docs.text:
This workflow demonstrates how to use the OpenAI LLM SDK to generate a response to a user's message.

Download Workflow

Note
  • Make sure that you have the OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable set in preferences.
  • Make sure that the output mode in the Text Node is set to LLM Content.
  • The exact index to access the response from a non-reasoning OpenAI response is ['output'][0]['content'][0]['text']. This is the value that you need to pass to the query with index node.

docs.title: OpenAI Vision Response
docs.text:
This workflow demonstrates how to use the OpenAI to generate a response to a user's message to use the vision capabilities of an LLM.

Download Workflow

Note
  • Make sure that you have the OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable set in preferences.
  • Make sure that the output mode in the Text and Image Nodes is set to LLM Content.
  • The exact index to access the response from a non-reasoning OpenAI response is ['output'][0]['content'][0]['text']. This is the value that you need to pass to the query with index node.

docs.title: OpenAI Response with Schema
docs.text:
This workflow demonstrates how to use the OpenAI to generate a response to a user's message with a schema.

Download Workflow

Note
  • Make sure that you have the OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable set in preferences.
  • Make sure that the output mode in the Text Node is set to LLM Content.
  • The exact index to access the response from a non-reasoning OpenAI response is ['parsed_output']. This is the value that you need to pass to the query with index node.

docs.text:
This workflow demonstrates how to use the OpenAI to generate a response to a user's message with a schema.

Download Workflow

Note
  • Make sure that you have the OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable set in preferences.
  • Make sure that the output mode in the Text and Image Nodes is set to LLM Content.
  • The exact index to access the response from a non-reasoning OpenAI response is ['parsed_output']. This is the value that you need to pass to the query with index node.
